title: Sequential tests: how to peek honestly
intro: In the previous lesson we saw that peeking at a regular A/B and stopping at the first "significant" inflates false wins. But the business wants to stop tests earlier. There is an honest way — sequential methods, which price in the cost of repeated checks up front.

beats.text: The main idea — this is the same day-by-day p-value chart as in the peeking lesson, except the stopping boundary is now honest. Choose "naive 0.05 threshold", the "no effect (A/A)" scenario, and run "+50 tests": the lines keep diving under the flat threshold — the share of false stops is far beyond 5% (that is the peeking from the previous lesson). Now switch to "sequential boundary (α-spending)": under the same A/A, the share returns to an honest ~5%, because the threshold is strict early in the test and relaxes only toward the planned day. That is the trick: the cost of repeated checks is budgeted in advance. Pocock and O'Brien–Fleming are specific families of such boundaries.
The naive rule — "fix the duration and look once at the end" — works, but slowly: a good effect has to be waited out to the end even if it shows early. You want to stop earlier — but honestly.
beats.predict: Can you allow many checks and still hold the overall false-positive risk at 5%?
beats.reveal: Yes — if you "distribute" the error budget across the checks in advance. That is exactly what sequential methods do: each interim check uses a stricter threshold, so the total Type I error stays at 5%. You pay in strictness for the right to look often.

beats.text: Two working families. Group sequential (O'Brien–Fleming, Pocock boundaries): planned interim "cuts" with tightened thresholds and the option to stop early — born in clinical trials. Always-valid p-values / mSPRT: special p-values and intervals you may look at ANY moment, as many times as you like.

Why the business cares: you can stop a clear win or a clear failure earlier without burning traffic, and without fooling yourself. The price is a somewhat larger required sample in the worst case and heavier math. That is why these methods are built into mature A/B platforms rather than computed by hand.

The takeaway: "no peeking" is about the REGULAR fixed-horizon test. If you need in-flight monitoring, don't eyeball a lower threshold — take a method built for it. The tool must match how you actually make the decision.

definitions.text: a method that allows repeated in-flight checks while controlling the overall Type I error.
definitions.simple: a design in which interim looks are permitted in advance.

definitions.text: planned interim cuts with tightened thresholds (O'Brien–Fleming, Pocock) and an early-stop option.
definitions.simple: several planned stopping points, each with its own error budget.

definitions.text: a p-value/interval you may look at any moment, any number of times, without inflating the error.
definitions.simple: a p-value you can trust whenever you look.

definitions.text: distributing the allowed Type I error across several checks so the total stays at 5%.
definitions.simple: 5% error is a budget: spend a piece on each check.

decision: If you want to watch mid-test, choose a sequential design before the start. Changing the stopping rules mid-test is not allowed.
practice: If the team needs to monitor a test and stop early, that is not a reason to break the statistics — it is a reason to switch on the sequential mode in the A/B platform. Then an early stop is honest, not self-deception.

Without such methods the rule is simple and hard: fix the sample size and the duration in advance, decide once at the end. Any "let's switch it off, it's already significant on day three" in a fixed-horizon test inflates false wins.
realLife: Clinical trials have used group sequential designs for decades: interim analyses make it ethical to stop a trial when a drug clearly works or clearly harms.
Modern product A/B platforms (including at major tech companies) offer always-valid statistics precisely so product managers can peek at the dashboard honestly.
assumptions: The speed gain is not free: in the worst case sequential methods require no smaller — and sometimes a larger — maximum sample; the saving is on average, not guaranteed.
You must use a method built for the purpose. Taking a regular fixed-horizon p-value and checking it daily is not a "sequential test" — it is exactly the peeking.
